output_text:
Reviewed an introduction/abstract section for a paper on a calibrated variant-pathogenicity model; below are concrete recommendations.

## Substantive issues

1. **Single-baseline comparison is too narrow.** The only comparator is the authors' own prior model (CalibVar-v1). For missense pathogenicity prediction there is a crowded field of established baselines — REVEL, AlphaMissense, EVE, PrimateAI, ESM1b, CADD, VARITY, MutPred2. A reviewer will reject the empirical claim ("substantial step forward") without at least the strongest 2–3 of these. Self-improvement over a prior version is not evidence of state-of-the-art.

2. **The "unaware of any prior method" claim is unsupportable as written.** Calibration of pathogenicity predictors has been studied (e.g., work on REVEL calibration for ACMG PP3/BP4 evidence strength by Pejaver et al., 2022). Either drop the claim, scope it precisely ("among deep ensemble methods on ClinVar-2024 at <N> parameters…"), or cite what you searched and why prior methods don't qualify.

3. **ClinVar circularity / data leakage is unaddressed.** ClinVar-2024 held-out split and gnomAD rare variants will overlap with training data of virtually any modern predictor, and ClinVar labels themselves are partially derived from in-silico tools. The introduction needs one sentence on how leakage was controlled (date-stratified split? gene holdout? variants absent from training-time ClinVar dump?). Otherwise the AUROC numbers are not interpretable.

4. **AUROC gains are small and significance is not reported.** 0.943 vs 0.928 on ClinVar, 0.901 vs 0.887 on gnomAD, 0.872 vs 0.859 on BRCA-Exchange — these deltas are in the range where bootstrap CIs frequently overlap. Report 95% CIs or a DeLong test; without them the "substantial step forward" framing is unearned.

5. **The high-confidence accuracy comparison is confounded by calibration itself.** "Among predictions with confidence > 0.9, CalibVar is correct 92.4% vs 84.1% for v1" is partly a tautology — temperature scaling reshuffles which predictions exceed 0.9. You should also report (a) the fraction of predictions above 0.9 for each model (coverage), and (b) accuracy at matched coverage, or a selective-prediction / risk-coverage curve. As written, v1 could be making more confident predictions and the comparison is not apples-to-apples.

6. **ECE averaging hides per-benchmark behavior.** "ECE drops from 0.064 to 0.019 averaged across three benchmarks" — report per-benchmark ECE. Averaging ECE across heterogeneous datasets is not standard and can mask a benchmark where calibration got worse. Also specify number of bins and whether equal-width or equal-mass.

7. **"Temperature-scaled deep ensemble" needs one more sentence of contribution framing.** Both deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) and temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017) are off-the-shelf. The introduction should state plainly what is novel: the application domain, a specific architectural change, the integration recipe, the calibration data, or the benchmark itself. Otherwise readers will read this as a straightforward application of two known techniques.

8. **Clinical utility claims overreach.** "Broadly useful as clinical genetics laboratories transition toward computationally assisted variant triage at population scale" — no clinical lab will adopt a tool from an AUROC table. Either soften to "could support" / "is designed to support," or cite a downstream evaluation (curator time saved, ACMG evidence-strength mapping per Pejaver-style framework, prospective lab pilot).

9. **"Fewer than 200 lines of code" is not a contribution.** Delete or move to the methods section. It distracts from substantive claims and invites skepticism (200 LOC on top of what?).

10. **Missing: definition of "confidence."** The piece uses "confidence" interchangeably with the predicted probability. Be explicit — for a binary classifier, confidence = max(p, 1-p) is conventional; given the focus on calibration, define this once.

## Smaller issues

- "Anonymous Authors et al., 2023" — fine for double-blind submission; confirm venue policy permits self-citation in this form.
- "Section 4" forward-reference is fine but verify the section number after revisions.
- "Emit point pathogenicity scores without confidence signals" overstates the prior art — many predictors output probabilities; the real distinction is *calibrated* probabilities. Tighten to "without calibration guarantees" or similar.
- BRCA-Exchange is a single-gene benchmark; either justify its inclusion (clinical relevance, distribution shift test) or note the limitation.
- Consider reporting Brier score alongside ECE — it is the proper scoring rule and less sensitive to binning choices.

## Suggested restructure

The current four paragraphs are: pitch, headline numbers, calibration drill-down, broader claims. Consider: (1) clinical problem + uncertainty gap, (2) what CalibVar does and what is specifically new, (3) headline results with CIs and at least one external baseline, (4) scope and limitations (single-institution training? gene coverage? leakage controls?). The current ordering buries the contribution and front-loads numbers that a reviewer cannot yet contextualize.

## Uncertainty disclosure

I have not verified: (a) whether CalibVar-v1 is a real prior publication or a placeholder, (b) the specific Pejaver et al. 2022 citation details (the paper exists, but confirm exact reference before adding), (c) which of the listed competing predictors (REVEL, AlphaMissense, etc.) are most appropriate baselines for your specific evaluation setup — that depends on training-data composition you have not described, (d) the venue's anonymization conventions.
